did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes an automatic skin lesion categorization system for melanoma detection using a pretrained deep learning network and transfer learning. The methodology includes preprocessing (CLAHE, top-hat transform), segmentation using a modified k-means clustering algorithm, feature extraction (GLCM, first-order statistics), and classification using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original automatic skin lesion categorization system using a pretrained deep learning network, transfer learning, modified k-means clustering, and CNNs. It details specific methodology (preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction) and reports experimental results comparing the proposed method's classification rate to existing approaches, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: ANN Based Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2022 IEEE International Interdisciplinary Humanitarian Conference for Sustainability (IIHC-2022)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automatic skin lesion categorization system for early melanoma detection using a pretrained deep learning network and transfer learning. The methodology involves preprocessing dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset with CLAHE and top-hat transforms, followed by segmentation using a modified k-means clustering algorithm. Features are extracted via first-order statistics and gray-level co-occurrence matrices (GLCM) from the segmented regions. These features are fed into a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify lesions as benign or malignant. The authors report that their proposed method achieves a higher classification rate compared to existing approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining texture-based feature extraction with CNNs for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on melanoma detection, classifying lesions as benign or malignant. The dataset (ISIC) contains images labeled as melanoma (fewer than 350 in the training set). The system distinguishes between benign and malignant melanoma/tumours.

The classification task involves distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions. The system outputs whether an image is benign, suspicious, or malignant. 'Normal skin' and 'healthy skin' are mentioned as the background or non-lesion class for segmentation purposes.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of skin cancer that can occur in children, but not part of the specific classification task or dataset used in the experiments.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an automatic skin lesion categorization system to distinguish between benign and malignant melanoma. The CNN classifier determines whether pixels or the overall image represent malignant or benign lesions. The system outputs discrete diagnostic categories: benign, suspicious, or malignant.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Dataset
datasets_used.description: The largest and most complete collection of dermoscopic images of skin lesions, checked for quality and available publicly. The study utilized a subset of this dataset for training, validation, and testing.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning model; images were preprocessed (CLAHE, top-hat), segmented (k-means), and augmented (rotation, cropping) to multiply the training set by 48.
use.validation: Used as part of the validation set (180 images) to evaluate the model.
use.testing: Used as part of the testing set (650 images) to evaluate the classification performance.
use.other: Segmentation masks were provided for each image to classify pixels as belonging to the lesion or healthy skin.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compares the proposed method's classification rate to existing approaches qualitatively, stating that the proposed method's rate 'exceeded the output of the existing approaches' and that hybrid CNNs show 'much better performance' and 'much better accuracy' compared to other networks, but provides no statistical significance metrics.

Proposed Method (CNN with GLCM and first.classification_rate: Exceeded the output of existing approaches

ISIC Dataset.notes: The paper states that the classification rate of the suggested method exceeded the output of existing approaches. It also notes that hybrid CNNs show 'much better performance' and 'much better accuracy' compared to other networks. No specific numerical accuracy values or percentages are reported for the proposed method's results on the ISIC dataset in the provided text.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained deep learning network (specific model not named, but transfer learning is used); mentions Hybrid CNN-IHHO with ResNet, VGG, and DenseNet in the context of detecting normal/abnormal melanoma tumours

architecture_details.structure: CNN constructed of three layers: a kernel, two convolution layers, and an output layer
components: Pooling layers (picks mean or maximum value to reduce dimension)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Gray-level co-occurrence matrices (GLCM) and first-order statistics extracted from segmented regions before being fed into the CNN

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation (0, 90, 180, 270 degrees), cropping, and synthetic image generation; training dataset multiplied by 48

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer / Neural Network feed-forward model
classification_or_output_head.output_description: Classifies pixels or images as malignant or benign based on texture and features

preprocessing_techniques: CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) to avoid noise amplification and enhance contrast by processing tiles with clip-limit and Rayleigh/exponential histogram parameters
Top-hat morphological transformation (black and white top-hat) to extract small elements, remove thick hair, and correct uneven lighting effects
Unsharp mask filter applied to images with low sharpness to make them sharper
Median filters, low pass filters, and high pass filters for noise reduction and image processing
Binary masking applied to the original image
Data augmentation via rotation (0, 90, 180, 270 degrees) and cropping to multiply the training dataset
Segmentation masks provided for each image to classify pixels as belonging to the lesion or healthy skin

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated by 0, 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
Images were cropped from the top left, top right, bottom left, and bottom right corners, then shrunk to original dimensions to generate multiple versions.
Synthetic images were generated from each input photograph; the training dataset was multiplied by 48 using these augmented versions.

conclusions: The proposed automatic skin lesion categorization system, which combines a pretrained deep learning network (CNN) with transfer learning, modified k-means clustering for segmentation, and feature extraction using GLCM and first-order statistics, effectively distinguishes between benign and malignant melanoma.
The classification rate of the suggested method exceeded the output of existing approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed pipeline.
Hybrid CNNs (specifically CNN-IHHO with ResNet, VGG, and DenseNet) show much better performance and accuracy compared to other networks for detecting normal and abnormal melanoma tumours.

Texture-based features extracted via GLCM are effective for skin cancer detection, particularly when combined with CNNs, as they help overcome challenges related to lighting effects and noise in dermoscopy images.
Preprocessing techniques such as CLAHE and top-hat transforms are necessary to remove noise, correct uneven lighting, and enhance image quality before segmentation and classification.
Data augmentation (rotation, cropping, and synthetic image generation) is critical to address the limited number of images in databases and to multiply the training dataset size.
Early identification of melanoma through automated systems significantly increases survival rates by enabling earlier diagnosis compared to relying solely on board-certified dermatologists.
important_remarks: The paper lacks specific quantitative performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity values) for the proposed method, relying only on qualitative claims that its classification rate 'exceeded the output of the existing approaches'.
The study uses a very small subset of the ISIC dataset, with fewer than 350 images labeled as melanoma in the training set, raising concerns about class imbalance and generalizability.
No statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the claimed superiority over existing methods.

The CNN architecture is described as having only three layers (kernel, two convolution layers, output), which is unusually shallow for modern deep learning tasks and may limit feature extraction capability.
The paper does not report key training hyperparameters such as the optimizer, loss function, learning rate, or number of epochs.

Data leakage risk exists as the paper mentions using segmentation masks provided in the dataset for pixel classification, but it is unclear if the same masks were used for training the segmentation component (modified k-means) or if this constitutes a form of supervised leakage in an unsupervised segmentation step.

The term 'racist characteristics' is used in the text to describe artifacts removed by preprocessing, which is scientifically imprecise and potentially misleading; it likely refers to skin tone variations or lighting artifacts.

The work is published in a conference proceeding (IIHC-2022) with limited peer review scope compared to major journals, and the methodology relies heavily on traditional image processing (GLCM, k-means) combined with a simple CNN, rather than state-of-the-art deep learning architectures.
External validation is not performed; the model is evaluated solely on the ISIC dataset subset, limiting clinical relevance assessment.
